lens.included_activities: A correctly classified non-bank holding-company office only if it independently supplies a recurring or repeat outsourced service to unaffiliated external customers while remaining primarily a non-managing holding office. The classification evidence suggests this is an exceptional mixed-activity case rather than the ordinary establishment model.
lens.excluded_activities: Entities whose primary activity is holding securities or other equity interests to own a controlling interest or influence management; offices that directly manage affiliated establishments; captive shared-service units; operating subsidiaries; shells; passive or non-control investment vehicles; and internal reorganizations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The default lens requires repeat service revenue from external customers. No defensible recurring external-customer billing model was identified for correctly classified other holding-company offices; ordinary economics arise from ownership interests and control rather than outsourced-service contracts.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for the broader Management of Companies and Enterprises sector, which combines passive or non-managing holding offices with active managing offices, and O*NET describes accounting and general-management tasks. I translate the prevalence of document, reporting, analysis, planning, and monitoring work into a bounded share of current tasks exposed to AI. The proxy likely overstates exposure for minimally staffed pure holding offices because managing offices contribute much of the observed employment, but may understate exposure where a small staff is concentrated in record review and reporting.
a.rationale: The broader sector contains substantial accounting, bookkeeping, financial-management, office-supervision, and general-management employment. O*NET shows that these roles review records and performance data, prepare reports, analyze controls, coordinate work, and make consequential decisions. Language and analytical components are exposed, while ownership judgment, governance, negotiation, and accountable approval constrain substitution.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit staffing census or payroll extract for other holding-company offices, paired with task-level time studies separating passive ownership administration from direct management and centralized services, would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupation evidence is for sector 55 rather than this six-digit code.
The broader sector includes managing offices that the Census form places in a different six-digit code.
The range is wage-weighted task exposure in concept, but public data do not provide a six-digit task-by-wage crosswalk.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures whether U.S. businesses use AI in any business function and near-term expected use, not the five-year implemented share of exposed tasks in other holding-company offices. I use the all-business and firm-size observations as an adoption anchor, then reduce implementation from technical exposure for data quality, integration, control, confidentiality, and change constraints. Larger professionally staffed holding groups may implement faster than the national average, while small or lightly staffed holding entities may implement less because there is little repeat workflow to automate.
rho.rationale: Current AI use is widespread enough to support material five-year workflow integration, especially in finance, administration, and reporting. But simple tool use does not demonstrate end-to-end implementation, and a holding office retains human responsibility for capital allocation, governance, negotiations, and exceptions.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of controllers, legal leaders, investment staff, and technology owners at other holding companies reporting production AI coverage by workflow, audit results, and measured labor-hour changes would materially change the range.
caveats: BTOS measures any business-function use, not task coverage or substitution.
No published NAICS 551112 adoption rate was identified.
The five-year implementation conversion is judgmental and sensitive to holding-company staffing intensity.

e.rationale: Eligibility is estimated near zero because the Census activity choices separate a holding company not engaged in direct management from a managing office. Ownership and influence are not recurring outsourced services to external customers. The modest positive tail allows for mixed-revenue or classification-edge establishments, which may be somewhat more varied than regulated bank holding companies, without treating them as representative.
e.change_evidence: A record-level business register with primary NAICS, affiliate status, external-customer service revenue, service descriptions, and normalized EBITDA for other holding-company offices would directly identify the eligible share.
caveats: No observed count of external-service sellers within this code was found.
Public company descriptions often consolidate holding entities and operating subsidiaries, obscuring establishment-level classification.
The supplied firm-count input may be materially misleading for this lens because it counts holding-company establishments rather than verified outsourced-service firms.

s5.rationale: No defensible eligible-firm cohort exists on which to measure qualifying control transfers. Transactions involving holding companies can represent portfolio acquisitions, changes above or below the establishment, internal restructurings, or transfers of operating subsidiaries rather than transfers of an eligible external-service operation.
s5.change_evidence: After an eligible cohort is identified, PitchBook, S&P Capital IQ, or Refinitiv transactions matched to establishment-level service revenue, seller identity, control terms, and post-close operating continuity could provide a five-year qualifying-transfer numerator and surviving-firm denominator.
caveats: Holding-company transaction volume is not a proxy for transfers of eligible service firms without deal-level classification.
Internal reorganizations and subsidiary sales must be excluded.

q.rationale: No recurring external-customer contracts, billing basis, renewal behavior, or competitive repricing evidence was identified for an eligible other holding-company service population. Retained gross benefit cannot be estimated from ownership income or intercompany allocations.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples and cohort-level gross-margin bridges from verified eligible establishments, including fee basis, renewal repricing, customer concessions, and competitor response after automation, would support this quantity.
caveats: Dividends, capital gains, and intercompany charges are not evidence of external service billing.
No fixed-fee, hourly, subscription, or cost-plus mix was observable for the frozen lens.

d5.rationale: The current external-service basket and its constant-price quantity are not observable because the code identifies a holding function rather than an outsourced-service market. There is no defensible denominator for a five-year demand ratio.
d5.change_evidence: Verified service-line revenue and unit-volume histories from eligible establishments, paired with external-customer budgets and forecast volumes, would establish current quantity and a five-year demand path.
caveats: Growth in portfolio companies, assets, or holding-company establishments does not directly measure demand for an external service basket.
Broader sector data combine holding and managing offices.

o.rationale: Without an identified external service basket or eligible operator population, there is no defensible basis for estimating what share of year-five quantity still requires an accountable operator of this kind rather than software, customer self-service, or another supplier.
o.change_evidence: Workflow-level customer research for verified eligible services, including willingness to self-serve, software substitution, liability allocation, and requirements for an accountable provider, would support this quantity.
caveats: Governance obligations within a corporate group do not themselves establish external customer demand.
Any estimate would depend on first defining and observing a qualifying service.

narrative.executive_view: The official classification is structurally at odds with the frozen outsourced-service lens. An other holding-company office principally owns equity interests to control or influence affiliated enterprises; the Census form separately identifies direct management and centralized support. Eligible external-service firms are therefore treated as rare mixed-activity or classification-edge cases, and unsupported commercial quantities remain missing.
narrative.ai_impact: Where a staffed office exists, AI can assist accounting review, performance reporting, document preparation, research, monitoring, and portions of planning. Human responsibility remains material for capital allocation, governance, negotiation, exceptions, and consequential approvals, so exposed work does not translate one-for-one into implemented substitution.
narrative.value_capture: No external-customer billing structure was found for correctly classified other holding-company offices. Internal efficiency may benefit owners of a corporate group, but ownership returns and captive allocations do not establish retention from outsourced-service customers under this lens.
narrative.firm_availability: The Census form explicitly distinguishes a holding company not engaged in direct management from an office that manages or provides centralized support to affiliated establishments. Most establishments in this code are therefore ineligible by business model, and the evidence does not reveal a transferable cohort of qualifying external-service firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Ownership administration and governance may persist as corporate functions, but their persistence is not evidence of external-customer demand for a recurring service basket. Current service quantity and the year-five need for an operator remain undefined until qualifying services are observed.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal risk is category error: treating ownership vehicles, captive functions, or portfolio-company transactions as independent service businesses. The occupation and adoption evidence also comes from broader populations and may substantially misstate the small, varied staff of a pure holding office.
narrative.principal_driver: Financial, accounting, reporting, and monitoring workflows in staffed holding offices contain meaningful document and analytical task exposure.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry definition supplies no observable recurring external-customer service model, leaving the eligible firm base and all commercial-demand primitives unsupported.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form MN-55111: Management of Companies and Enterprises
sources.supports: The primary-activity choices distinguish non-managing other holding companies from bank and financial holding companies and from offices engaged in direct management or centralized administrative support.

sources.title: Management of Companies and Enterprises: NAICS 55
sources.supports: Defines the broader sector, explains its mix of holding and managing establishments, and reports common accounting, financial-management, office-supervision, and general-management occupations.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 551000
sources.supports: Provides the detailed occupational distribution for the broader Management of Companies and Enterprises sector used as the occupation-mix proxy.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Accountants and Auditors
sources.supports: Describes accounting work including examining records, preparing statements and reports, analyzing controls, and advising management, which informs the task-exposure proxy.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: General and Operations Managers
sources.supports: Describes performance-data review, financial and operational coordination, policy work, planning, and high-frequency consequential decision making, informing the exposure and human-accountability bounds.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports recent U.S. business AI adoption by firm size and sector, used as a broad anchor for the implementation range.
